user_story: As a configuration manager, I want to list and filter CIs by lifecycle, status, and type so that I can review the configuration estate.
expected_behavior: Query params are validated optionally against ciFiltersSchema (lifecycle PLANNING..RETIRE, status PLANNED..DISPOSED, ciType, page>=1 default 1, limit 1..1000 default 50). It runs a Neo4j MATCH (ci:CI) with optional WHERE on itil_lifecycle/itil_config_status/type, computes a total count, then returns the page ordered by name (limit hard-capped at 1000). Response is {success,data:[CI...],pagination:{total,page,limit,pages}} with CI keys de-underscored via convertNeo4jCI; errors return 500.

user_story: As a configuration manager, I want to fetch one CI by id so that I can inspect its ITIL attributes.
expected_behavior: Calls neo4jClient.getCI(id); if not found returns 404 {success:false,error:'Not Found'}. On success returns {success:true,data} with the CI converted via convertNeo4jCI (underscore-prefixed keys stripped). Unexpected failures return 500.

user_story: As a configuration manager, I want to advance a CI through its ITIL lifecycle so that its stage reflects reality.
expected_behavior: Body.stage is required and must be one of PLANNING, DESIGN, BUILD, TEST, DEPLOY, OPERATE, RETIRE (updateLifecycleSchema); invalid values are rejected 400 by validateRequest. A Cypher MATCH (ci:CI {id}) SET ci.itil_lifecycle and ci.updated_at runs; 0 matched records returns 404, otherwise {success,data:CI,message}. No ci_history row is written by this handler.

user_story: As a configuration manager, I want to set a CI's configuration status so that operational state is tracked.
expected_behavior: Body.status is required and constrained to PLANNED, ORDERED, IN_DEVELOPMENT, ACTIVE, MAINTENANCE, RETIRED, DISPOSED (updateConfigStatusSchema). A Cypher SET ci.itil_config_status + updated_at runs; no matched record returns 404, success returns {success,data:CI,message}. Writes only to the Neo4j node; no history record is created here.

user_story: As an auditor, I want to view a CI's change history so that I can trace configuration changes over time.
expected_behavior: Reads the Postgres ci_history table WHERE ci_id=$1 ORDER BY changed_at DESC LIMIT (query.limit, default 100), returning {success,data:rows,count}. Note: the lifecycle and status PATCH handlers above write only to Neo4j and never insert into ci_history, so unless history is populated by an external trigger/service this endpoint may not reflect those mutations.

user_story: As an auditor, I want to see which CIs are due for audit so that I can plan compliance reviews.
expected_behavior: Runs a Neo4j MATCH (ci:CI) WHERE itil_next_audit_date <= now OR itil_last_audited IS NULL, ordered by next audit date, LIMIT 100, returning {success,data:[CI...],count}. The route is registered after /configuration-items/:id but does not collide because :id only captures a single path segment while audit/due is two segments.

user_story: As an auditor, I want to schedule an audit for a CI so that compliance reviews are tracked and assigned.
expected_behavior: Body validated by scheduleAuditSchema: auditDate (ISO date, required), auditor and notes optional. Cypher SET ci.itil_next_audit_date=datetime(auditDate), itil_audit_scheduled_by, itil_audit_notes, updated_at; no matched record returns 404, success returns {success,data:CI,message:'Audit scheduled successfully'}.

user_story: As an auditor, I want to record the outcome of a CI audit so that compliance status is captured.
expected_behavior: Body validated by completeAuditSchema: auditStatus required (COMPLIANT|NON_COMPLIANT|UNKNOWN), findings optional, completedBy required. Cypher SET ci.itil_audit_status, itil_last_audited=datetime(), itil_audit_findings, itil_audit_completed_by, updated_at; 404 if no CI matched, else {success,data:CI,message}. The audit status is persisted on the Neo4j CI node (not the Postgres ci_snapshot table the accuracy metric reads).

user_story: As a service desk analyst, I want to log an incident against an affected CI so that it is prioritised and tracked.
expected_behavior: Body validated by createIncidentSchema: affectedCIId required, description required min 10 chars, reportedBy required, symptoms array optional, detectedAt optional ISO. Verifies the CI exists in Neo4j (404 if not). Priority is computed by placeholder calculateBasicPriority (default P3/MEDIUM; production env -> P2/HIGH; type database/load-balancer/service -> P1/CRITICAL/HIGH urgency). Inserts into Postgres itil_incidents with id=incident_number=`INC-${Date.now()}`, status NEW, symptoms JSON-stringified, returning 201 {success,data:row,priorityCalculation,message}.

user_story: As an incident manager, I want to list and filter incidents so that I can triage the queue.
expected_behavior: Query optionally validated by incidentFiltersSchema (status NEW..CLOSED, priority 1..5, affectedCIId, page, limit). Builds a dynamic WHERE on status/priority/affected_ci_id against Postgres itil_incidents, computes total count, returns the page ordered by reported_at DESC (limit capped at 1000) as {success,data,pagination}.

user_story: As an incident manager, I want to fetch an incident by id so that I can review its full record.
expected_behavior: SELECT * FROM itil_incidents WHERE id=$1; if no rows returns 404, otherwise {success,data:row}. Errors return 500.

user_story: As an incident manager, I want to reassign, re-prioritise, or change incident status so that the record stays current.
expected_behavior: Body validated by updateIncidentSchema: status (NEW..CLOSED), assignedTo, priority (int 1..5) all optional. Builds a dynamic UPDATE; updated_at=NOW() is always added, and if no real fields were supplied (updates.length===1) it returns 400 'No fields to update'. 404 if the id matches no row, otherwise {success,data:row,message}.

user_story: As an incident manager, I want to resolve an incident with a resolution note so that MTTR can be measured.
expected_behavior: Body validated by resolveIncidentSchema: resolution required min 10 chars, resolvedBy required. UPDATE itil_incidents SET status='RESOLVED', resolution, resolved_by, resolved_at=NOW(), updated_at=NOW() WHERE id; 404 if no row, else {success,data:row,message}. resolved_at feeds the MTTR metric. No state-machine guard: an already CLOSED/RESOLVED incident can be resolved again.

user_story: As a service desk analyst, I want to see an incident's priority, impact, and urgency so that I understand its severity.
expected_behavior: SELECT priority, impact, urgency FROM itil_incidents WHERE id=$1; 404 if no row, otherwise {success,data:{priority,impact,urgency}}. Read-only; does not recompute priority.

user_story: As a change requester, I want to raise a change with implementation and backout plans so that it can be assessed and approved.
expected_behavior: Body validated by createChangeSchema: changeType (STANDARD|NORMAL|EMERGENCY), description min 10, affectedCIIds (min 1), requestedBy, plannedStart (ISO), plannedDuration (int minutes >=1), implementationPlan (min 50 chars), backoutPlan/testPlan optional. Each affected CI is verified to exist in Neo4j (404 on first missing). Inserts into Postgres itil_changes with id=change_number=`CHG-${Date.now()}`, affected_ci_ids JSON-stringified, status REQUESTED, returning 201 {success,data:row,message}.

user_story: As a change manager, I want to list and filter changes by status, type, and requester so that I can manage the change pipeline.
expected_behavior: Query optionally validated by changeFiltersSchema (status REQUESTED..CANCELLED, changeType STANDARD|NORMAL|EMERGENCY, requestedBy, page, limit). Builds a dynamic WHERE against itil_changes, computes total, returns the page ordered by requested_at DESC (limit capped 1000) as {success,data,pagination}.

user_story: As a change manager, I want to fetch a change by id so that I can review its plans and status.
expected_behavior: SELECT * FROM itil_changes WHERE id=$1; 404 if no row, otherwise {success,data:row}. Errors return 500.

user_story: As a change manager, I want to update a change's status, implementer, and actual duration so that the record reflects progress.
expected_behavior: Body validated by updateChangeSchema: status (REQUESTED..CANCELLED), implementedBy, actualDuration (int >=1) all optional. Dynamic UPDATE with updated_at=NOW() always appended; if no real field supplied returns 400 'No fields to update'; 404 if id matches no row; else {success,data:row,message}.

user_story: As a CAB member, I want a risk assessment for a change so that I can decide whether it needs CAB approval.
expected_behavior: Fetches the change (404 if missing) then computes a placeholder risk via calculateBasicRisk: parses affected_ci_ids; base riskScore 0.5/MEDIUM, >10 CIs -> 0.8/HIGH, >5 -> 0.6/MEDIUM, EMERGENCY adds +0.2 (capped 1.0) and forces HIGH; requiresCABApproval = riskScore>0.6. Returns {success,data:{riskScore,riskLevel,requiresCABApproval,affectedCICount,estimatedDowntime,estimatedUserImpact,estimatedRevenueAtRisk,recommendations,mitigationStrategies}}. Read-only; result is not persisted (TODO: real ChangeRiskService).

user_story: As a CAB member, I want to approve a change so that it can proceed to implementation.
expected_behavior: No request-body validation. UPDATE itil_changes SET status='APPROVED', updated_at=NOW() WHERE id; 404 if no row, else {success,data:row,message}. There is no precondition guard (a change in any status, even CLOSED, can be flipped to APPROVED) and no approver identity or approval timestamp is recorded.

user_story: As a change implementer, I want to mark a change as implemented so that its implementation time is recorded.
expected_behavior: No body validation. UPDATE itil_changes SET status='IMPLEMENTED', implemented_at=NOW(), updated_at=NOW() WHERE id; 404 if no row, else {success,data:row,message}. No precondition check that the change was APPROVED first.

user_story: As a change manager, I want to close a change with an outcome so that the change-success rate can be measured.
expected_behavior: Body validated by closeChangeSchema: result required (SUCCESS|PARTIAL_SUCCESS|FAILED|ROLLED_BACK), notes optional, closedBy required. UPDATE itil_changes SET status='CLOSED', result, closure_notes, closed_by, closed_at=NOW(), updated_at=NOW(); 404 if no row, else {success,data:row,message}. The result value drives the change-success-rate metric.

user_story: As a configuration manager, I want to snapshot a set of CIs as a baseline so that I can detect drift later.
expected_behavior: Body validated by createBaselineSchema: name (3-100 chars), ciIds (min 1), description optional, createdBy required. Each CI is verified to exist in Neo4j (404 on first missing). Inserts into Postgres itil_baselines with id=uuidv4(), ci_ids JSON-stringified, snapshot_date=NOW(), returning 201 {success,data:row,message}. Note: only the list of ci_ids is stored, not a snapshot of each CI's attribute values, which limits later comparison/restore.

user_story: As a configuration manager, I want to list recent baselines so that I can choose one to compare or restore.
expected_behavior: SELECT * FROM itil_baselines ORDER BY snapshot_date DESC LIMIT 100, returning {success,data:rows,count}. No pagination params; capped at 100. Errors return 500.

user_story: As a configuration manager, I want to fetch a baseline by id so that I can inspect its captured CIs.
expected_behavior: SELECT * FROM itil_baselines WHERE id=$1; 404 if no row, otherwise {success,data:row}. Errors return 500.

user_story: As a configuration manager, I want to delete an obsolete baseline so that the list stays relevant.
expected_behavior: DELETE FROM itil_baselines WHERE id=$1 RETURNING id; if nothing was deleted returns 404, otherwise responds 204 No Content with an empty body. Errors return 500.

user_story: As a configuration manager, I want to compare current CI state to a baseline so that I can identify configuration drift.
expected_behavior: Currently a stub: it ignores the baseline contents and returns 200 {success:true,data:{baselineId,comparisonDate,driftedCIs:[],totalDriftCount:0,driftPercentage:0},message:'Baseline comparison feature coming soon'}. No actual comparison against the stored snapshot is performed.
issue_note: compareToBaseline is unimplemented (itil.controller.ts:1236-1262) yet returns success:true with hardcoded empty drift, so callers receive a fake 'no drift' result.

user_story: As a configuration manager, I want to restore a CI's attributes from a baseline so that I can revert unwanted changes.
expected_behavior: Body is validated by restoreFromBaselineSchema (ciId required, restoreAttributes optional, performedBy required), but the handler ignores both params and body (they are commented out) and is a stub returning 200 {success:true,message:'Baseline restoration feature coming soon'}. No CI attributes are actually restored.
issue_note: restoreFromBaseline is unimplemented (itil.controller.ts:1264-1284); it returns success with no restoration and never reads the validated ciId/performedBy body.

user_story: As a service owner, I want a configuration accuracy percentage so that I can gauge how compliant audited CIs are.
expected_behavior: Queries Postgres ci_snapshot for COUNT(*) FILTER (itil_audit_status='COMPLIANT') over COUNT(*) WHERE itil_audit_status IS NOT NULL, returns {success,data:{accuracy(2dp string),compliantCount,totalAudited}} with totalAudited defaulting to 1 to avoid divide-by-zero. The data source is mismatched: audit completion (ITIL-008) writes itil_audit_status onto the Neo4j CI node, not the ci_snapshot table read here.
issue_note: getConfigurationAccuracy reads itil_audit_status from Postgres ci_snapshot (itil.controller.ts:1295-1301) but completeAudit persists itil_audit_status only on the Neo4j CI node (itil.controller.ts:338-342), so the metric never reflects completed audits.

user_story: As an incident manager, I want incident counts grouped by status and priority so that I can see the shape of the queue.
expected_behavior: SELECT status, priority, COUNT(*) FROM itil_incidents GROUP BY status, priority ORDER BY priority, status, returning {success,data:rows}. Errors return 500.

user_story: As a change manager, I want the percentage of successful closed changes so that I can track change quality.
expected_behavior: Over closed changes, computes COUNT(result='SUCCESS') / COUNT(status='CLOSED') * 100, returning {success,data:{successRate(2dp string),successfulChanges,totalClosedChanges}} with denominator defaulting to 1 to avoid divide-by-zero. Errors return 500.

user_story: As a service owner, I want the average incident resolution time so that I can monitor responsiveness.
expected_behavior: Computes AVG(EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (resolved_at - reported_at))/3600) over itil_incidents WHERE status='RESOLVED' AND resolved_at IS NOT NULL, returning {success,data:{mttr(2dp string),unit:'hours'}}; defaults to 0 when there are no resolved incidents. Errors return 500.

user_story: As a reliability engineer, I want the average time between incidents per CI so that I can assess CI stability.
expected_behavior: Uses a window function (LAG over reported_at PARTITION BY affected_ci_id) to compute gaps between consecutive incidents for each CI, then AVG(EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM gap)/3600) over non-null gaps, returning {success,data:{mtbf(2dp string),unit:'hours'}}; defaults to 0 when no CI has two or more incidents. Errors return 500.

user_story: As an API consumer, I want to query CIs with ITIL filters via GraphQL so that I can build dashboards.
expected_behavior: Runs the same Neo4j MATCH (ci:CI) filter as the REST list but returns a connection {items,total,page,limit,pages} of raw ci.properties (keys NOT de-underscored, unlike REST convertNeo4jCI). Notably ciType is normalised with toLowerCase().replace(/_/g,'-') before matching, so 'LOAD_BALANCER' becomes 'load-balancer'. On failure throws a GraphQLError with code INTERNAL_SERVER_ERROR.

user_story: As an API consumer, I want to fetch a single CI via GraphQL so that I can resolve its ITIL attributes and relations.
expected_behavior: Loads the CI through context._loaders._ciLoader (DataLoader, batched/cached). Returns the CI object (used by CI field resolvers itilAttributes/history/incidents/changes) or throws a GraphQLError on failure.

user_story: As an API consumer, I want a CI's history via GraphQL so that I can display its change log.
expected_behavior: SELECT * FROM ci_history WHERE ci_id=$1 ORDER BY changed_at DESC LIMIT (limit||100), returning the rows. Throws a GraphQLError on failure. Same ci_history dependency as the REST history endpoint.

user_story: As an API consumer, I want CIs due for audit via GraphQL so that I can surface them in tooling.
expected_behavior: Neo4j MATCH (ci:CI) WHERE itil_next_audit_date <= now OR itil_last_audited IS NULL ORDER BY itil_next_audit_date LIMIT (limit||100), returning ci.properties. Throws a GraphQLError on failure.

user_story: As an API consumer, I want configuration accuracy via GraphQL so that I can chart compliance.
expected_behavior: Same query as the REST metric: COUNT(itil_audit_status='COMPLIANT')/COUNT(itil_audit_status IS NOT NULL) over Postgres ci_snapshot, returning {accuracy(number),compliantCount,totalAudited,timestamp}. Shares the same data-source mismatch as the REST endpoint since audit completion writes to the Neo4j node, not ci_snapshot.
issue_note: GraphQL configurationAccuracy reads itil_audit_status from ci_snapshot (itil.resolvers.ts:135-141) which audit completion never populates, so accuracy will not reflect real audits.

user_story: As an API consumer, I want to query incidents via GraphQL so that I can build incident views.
expected_behavior: Builds a dynamic WHERE on status/priority/affected_ci_id against itil_incidents, computes total, returns connection {items,total,page,limit,pages} ordered by reported_at DESC. Throws a GraphQLError on failure. No Joi validation layer (GraphQL schema types gate inputs instead).

user_story: As an API consumer, I want a single incident via GraphQL so that I can resolve its CI and symptom fields.
expected_behavior: SELECT * FROM itil_incidents WHERE id=$1, returning rows[0] or null (no GraphQL error for not-found). Field resolvers expose affectedCI (via _ciLoader), symptoms (JSON-parsed), and affectedBusinessServices.

user_story: As an API consumer, I want aggregated incident metrics via GraphQL so that I can render an incidents dashboard in one call.
expected_behavior: Runs five queries in parallel (Promise.all): total count, counts by status, counts by priority, MTTR (avg resolved-minus-reported hours for RESOLVED), and MTBF (avg LAG gap per affected_ci_id). Returns {totalIncidents,byStatus,byPriority,mttr,mtbf}. Throws a GraphQLError if any query fails.

user_story: As an API consumer, I want to query changes via GraphQL so that I can build change views.
expected_behavior: Dynamic WHERE on status/change_type/requested_by against itil_changes, computes total, returns connection {items,total,page,limit,pages} ordered by requested_at DESC. Throws a GraphQLError on failure.

user_story: As an API consumer, I want a single change via GraphQL so that I can resolve its affected CIs and risk.
expected_behavior: SELECT * FROM itil_changes WHERE id=$1, returning rows[0] or null. Field resolvers expose affectedCIs (loaded via _ciLoader from parsed affected_ci_ids) and riskAssessment.

user_story: As an API consumer, I want aggregated change metrics via GraphQL so that I can render a change dashboard.
expected_behavior: Parallel queries for total changes, successful/failed counts among CLOSED changes, and per-type breakdown. Returns {totalChanges,successfulChanges,failedChanges,successRate,byType[{changeType,count,successRate}]}; successRate uses successful/(successful+failed) (closed-only denominator) rather than all closed changes. Throws a GraphQLError on failure.

user_story: As an API consumer, I want to query baselines via GraphQL so that I can list and inspect snapshots.
expected_behavior: baselines: SELECT * FROM itil_baselines ORDER BY snapshot_date DESC LIMIT (limit||100). baseline: SELECT * FROM itil_baselines WHERE id=$1 returning rows[0] or null. Both throw GraphQLError on query failure. The ConfigurationBaseline.cis field resolver loads each CI in ci_ids via _ciLoader.

user_story: As an API consumer, I want baseline drift via GraphQL so that I can show configuration drift.
expected_behavior: A stub: returns {baseline:(the looked-up baseline),comparisonDate,driftedCIs:[],totalDriftCount:0,driftPercentage:0} without performing any real comparison (TODO in code). Always reports zero drift.
issue_note: baselineComparison is unimplemented (itil.resolvers.ts:402-411); it always returns empty driftedCIs and zero drift counts.

user_story: As an API consumer, I want to update a CI's lifecycle stage or configuration status via GraphQL so that I can drive ITIL state from clients.
expected_behavior: Each runs a Cypher SET on the matching CI (itil_lifecycle or itil_config_status plus updated_at), then clears the DataLoader cache (_ciLoader.clear(id)) and reloads/returns the updated CI. Throws a GraphQLError on failure. Unlike REST there is no Joi enum validation here, so the schema's enum types are the only guard.

user_story: As an API consumer, I want to create and progress incidents, changes, and baselines via GraphQL so that I do not have to fall back to REST for writes.
expected_behavior: Only updateLifecycleStage and updateConfigurationStatus are implemented in the Mutation map. createIncident, updateIncident, resolveIncident, createChange, updateChange, approveChange, implementChange, closeChange, createBaseline, deleteBaseline, and restoreFromBaseline are left as a comment ('omitted for brevity') and are not implemented, so any such GraphQL mutations declared in the schema have no resolver.
issue_note: GraphQL ITIL write mutations beyond the two CI updates are unimplemented (itil.resolvers.ts:454-457), so incident/change/baseline writes are REST-only despite the resolver file claiming the GraphQL contract.

user_story: As an API consumer, I want a CI's ITIL attributes as a nested object so that I can read class, lifecycle, status, version, baseline, and audit info together.
expected_behavior: Maps the raw CI node's itil_* properties into {ciClass,lifecycleStage,configurationStatus,version,baselineId,lastAudited,auditStatus,nextAuditDate}, each defaulting to null when absent. Pure transformation, no DB call.

user_story: As an API consumer, I want a CI's related incidents, changes, and history resolved inline so that I can traverse the graph from a CI.
expected_behavior: CI.incidents queries itil_incidents WHERE affected_ci_id=parent.id (optional status filter, limit default 50, ordered by reported_at DESC). CI.changes queries itil_changes WHERE affected_ci_ids::jsonb ? parent.id (jsonb containment) with optional status filter, limit default 50. CI.history queries ci_history by ci_id, limit default 100. All return rows directly.

user_story: As an API consumer, I want the business services impacted by an incident so that I can assess business impact.
expected_behavior: Currently a stub that always returns an empty array (TODO: implement business service lookup), so business-impact data is never surfaced for an incident regardless of CI relationships.
issue_note: Incident.affectedBusinessServices always returns [] (itil.resolvers.ts:527-530); business-service impact is never computed.

user_story: As an API consumer, I want a change's risk assessment inline via GraphQL so that I can show risk alongside the change.
expected_behavior: Currently a stub that always returns null (TODO), even though the equivalent REST endpoint (ITIL-019) computes a basic risk assessment. GraphQL clients therefore cannot obtain change risk through this field.
issue_note: Change.riskAssessment always returns null (itil.resolvers.ts:541-544) while REST GET /changes/:id/risk-assessment does compute one, leaving the GraphQL field non-functional.
